summary: This ultra-short post shares a humorous pie chart joke where the chart itself visually resembles Ms. Pac-Man. Atwood uses this as a springboard to make a quick observation about early video game design conventions, specifically how female characters were distinguished from male ones simply by adding a bow. The post is essentially a visual gag with a brief cultural commentary on 8-bit era gender representation in games.
key_points: Pie charts can accidentally (or intentionally) resemble classic video game characters
Early video game design used minimal visual cues like bows to indicate female characters
The 'Ms.' prefix plus a bow was the standard formula for creating female versions of male game characters
money_quotes: In the innocent era of 8-bit arcades, you made characters female by adding a cute little pixelated red bow.
key_insight: Early video game design relied on the simplest possible visual shorthand—a red bow—to communicate gender, reflecting both the technical constraints and cultural assumptions of the 8-bit era.
